steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence sets up a logical inference: neither Geoffrey's History nor any work predating it mentions the Round Table, yet Malory's Le Morte d'Arthur does. The blank must complete the logical "suggesting that ______" conclusion.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Geoffrey of Monmouth's accounts of Arthurian legends in *History* are more similar overall in content to the accounts in *Culhwch* and *Olwen* than they are to the accounts in *Le Morte d'Arthur*.: the passage never compares the content similarity of these texts, so this is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice B — when a version of an Arthurian legend contradicted the version in *History*, Malory preferred to include Geoffrey of Monmouth's version in *Le Morte d'Arthur*.: the passage says Malory compiled contradictory sources, not that he favored Geoffrey's version over others. ✗
  • Choice C — *Le Morte d'Arthur* is more historically accurate than *History* because *Culhwch* and *Olwen* had not been written when Geoffrey of Monmouth was writing his work.: historical accuracy is never discussed, and the timeline claim doesn't follow from the Round Table evidence. ✗
  • Choice D — Malory encountered the Round Table in a source that Geoffrey of Monmouth was not familiar with when writing his *History*.: directly follows from the evidence. The Round Table is absent from History and all earlier works, so Malory must have drawn it from a source outside Geoffrey's sphere — consistent with the note that only many (not all) of Malory's sources derive from Geoffrey. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
